coredata.dc:title: Great-enhanced performance of Pt nanoparticles by the unique carbon quantum dot/reduced graphene oxide hybrid supports towards methanol electrochemical oxidation
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Tian-Zeng
Xue, Qiong
Yang, Zhi-Yong
Dong, Ya-Ping
coredata.dc:description: The Pt-carbon quantum dot (CQD)/reduced graphene oxide (RGO) catalysts are prepared by one pot reduction method and demonstrate ultraefficient performance towards methanol oxidation reaction (MOR). In the high content CQD products, Pt nanoparticles around 2–3 nm are dispersed uniformly on supporting materials. And the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis indicates that in the high content CQD products a large part of surface oxygen groups is contributed by CQD. The electrochemical tests reveal that the catalyst with the saturated CQD exhibits best performance in MOR: the mass and specific activity at forward peak position, the potential close to fuel cell operation and 3600 s of chronoamperometric curve are roughly 2–3 folds of the commercial Pt/C. Furthermore, the electrochemical data on the series of catalysts with different quantity of CQD disclose the improving tendency of MOR performance with the increasing content of CQD evidently. Overview the electrochemical and characterization results, we suggest CQD play multiple roles in the enhancement of Pt performance: present abundant nucleating and anchoring points to facilitate the formation of small size and uniform distributed Pt particles; act as spacer to alleviate restacking of RGO sheets; and provide fruitful surface oxygen groups to improve the antipoisonous ability of Pt.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pt catalyst
Carbon quantum dot
Graphene
Methanol oxidation reaction
Fuel cell

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the catalyst with saturated amount of CQD (Pt-CQD/RGO)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQD; (b) PL spectra of CQD. Excited at 362 nm.
Large scale (a) and high resolution (b) SEM image of Pt-CQD/RGO; Large scale (c) and high resolution (d) SEM image of Pt-RGO.
Large scale (a) and high resolution (b) TEM image of Pt-CQD/RGO; Large scale (c) and high resolution (d) TEM image of Pt-RGO.

(a) XRD patterns of Pt-CQD/RGO (black) and Pt-RGO (light cyan); (b) Raman spectra of GO (yellow), Pt-CQD/RGO (black) and Pt-RGO (light cyan). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of Pt-CQD/RGO (black), Pt-RGO (light cyan) and Pt/C (red) in 0.5 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (b) the CV curves of Pt-CQD/RGO (black), Pt-RGO (light cyan) and Pt/C (red) in 0.5 M H2SO4 + 1 M CH3OH with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (c) the chronoamperometric (I-t) curves of Pt-CQD/RGO (black), Pt-RGO (light cyan) and Pt/C (red) in 0.5 M H2SO4 + 1 M CH3OH at 0.50 V (vs SCE); Insert: Zoomed-in I-t curves at last stage. All the curves in Figure 5 are normalized by the loading mass (mg) of Pt. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM (a) and TEM (b) image of Pt-CQD/RGO-5; SEM (c) and TEM (d) image of Pt-CQD/RGO-30.

(a) The CV curves of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 (green), Pt-CQD/RGO-30 (blue) and Pt-CQD/RGO (black) in 0.5 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (b) the CV curves of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 (green), Pt-CQD/RGO-30 (blue) and Pt-CQD/RGO (black) in 0.5 M H2SO4 + 1 M CH3OH with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (c) the chronoamperometric curves of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 (green), Pt-CQD/RGO-30 (blue) and Pt-CQD/RGO (black) in 0.5 M H2SO4 + 1 M CH3OH at 0.50 V (vs SCE); Insert: Zoomed-in I-t curves at last stage. All curves in Figure 7 are normalized by loading mass (mg) of Pt. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The electrochemical data of all the tested catalysts in this report.

head.ce:title: Great-enhanced performance of Pt nanoparticles by the unique carbon quantum dot/reduced graphene oxide hybrid supports towards methanol electrochemical oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19A Yuquanlu, Beijing, 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Tian-Zeng Hong and Qiong Xue contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Pt-carbon quantum dot (CQD)/reduced graphene oxide (RGO) catalysts are prepared by one pot reduction method and demonstrate ultraefficient performance towards methanol oxidation reaction (MOR). In the high content CQD products, Pt nanoparticles around 2–3 nm are dispersed uniformly on supporting materials. And the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis indicates that in the high content CQD products a large part of surface oxygen groups is contributed by CQD. The electrochemical tests reveal that the catalyst with the saturated CQD exhibits best performance in MOR: the mass and specific activity at forward peak position, the potential close to fuel cell operation and 3600 s of chronoamperometric curve are roughly 2–3 folds of the commercial Pt/C. Furthermore, the electrochemical data on the series of catalysts with different quantity of CQD disclose the improving tendency of MOR performance with the increasing content of CQD evidently. Overview the electrochemical and characterization results, we suggest CQD play multiple roles in the enhancement of Pt performance: present abundant nucleating and anchoring points to facilitate the formation of small size and uniform distributed Pt particles; act as spacer to alleviate restacking of RGO sheets; and provide fruitful surface oxygen groups to improve the antipoisonous ability of Pt.

ce:section.ce:para: The direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) is considered as one of most promising clean power source because of its high energy density, relative quick feedback to loading variation, easy handling and storing liquid fuel. However, the high cost of most widely used Pt-based catalysts limits the total loading amount of Pt in each DMFC to keep the cost of fuel cell acceptable for commercialization. Thus, the Pt catalysts have to be highly active and can maintain their sufficient activity in long term. Currently, the performance of Pt-based catalysts remains large distance from these critical requirements. To improve their utilization and avoid aggregation, Pt nanoparticles are usually dispersed on carbon materials with good conductivity and high surface area [1,2].

Recently, graphene and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) are intensely researched as supporting materials of Pt nanoparticles due to their distinctive properties such as, high surface area, tunable surface functional groups, excellent conductivity and compatible producing methods with the reduction of Pt precursors [3,4]. Most of the synthesized Pt catalysts demonstrate improving performance than the used standard samples. Conductive polymer, carbon nanotube (CNT), mesoporous carbon, even graphitic carbon nitrides are employed to construct hybrid supporting materials with RGO [5–8]. Usually, the surface area of the prepared RGO hybrids is increased further because the added components prevent RGO from restacking efficiently, thus the activities of the corresponding catalysts are boosted. And the long term stability may benefit from the enhanced antisegregation of the hybrid substrates as well.

Besides the surface area, it is recognized in recent researches that the surface groups of carbon materials, especially the oxygen-containing groups, play a key role in improving the activities of Pt catalysts in both methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Therefore, as oxygen group enriched materials, graphene quantum dots (GQD) and carbon quantum dots (CQD) are introduced to the fuel cell catalysts [9,10]. To date, most of the reported fuel cell catalysts employed GQD or CQD as supporting materials or as Pt-free catalysts were investigated in ORR and show excellent performance [11–25]. Very few research reports the GQD or CQD-containing catalysts for MOR except in a very recent reference which indicates that the Pt loading on N-doped CQD/CNT hybrid is more active towards MOR than only on CNT [26]. CQD carries abundant oxygen groups [27] on its surface and may prevent RGO from restacking also, which provides a considerable opportunity to promote the performance of Pt catalysts to MOR largely. But this point has not been paid enough attentions in current researches.

Here, in this report, the catalysts made by Pt nanoparticles supported on the CQD/RGO hybrids are synthesized by one pot reduction of CQD, graphene oxide (GO) and Pt precursor with ethylene glycol (EG). The MOR tests reveal that the fulfillments of the catalyst with the saturated amount of CQD are best optimized. And the systematical electrochemical data acquired on the catalysts with varied content of CQD disclose the obvious relevancy between the improving performance and the increasing quantity of CQD. The results in our research indicate that the careful designed substrate, especially the surface properties of substrate, can indeed promote the achievements of Pt catalysts distinctively and provide an alternative way to develop low cost but efficient catalysts for fuel cells.

GO was prepared by oxidation of natural graphite powders (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.) through a modified Hummer's method and purified by dialysis [28,29]. CQD was synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 0.1 M Vitamin C (Sigma Aldrich Co.) solution at 180 °C for 1.5 h. The produced solution was purified by centrifugation and the supernatant solution was dried completely in vacuum oven to get the CQD powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the catalyst with saturated amount of CQD (Pt-CQD/RGO)
ce:section.ce:para: 60 mg CQD, 60 mg GO and 35 mg H2PtCl6 (Sigma Aldrich Co.) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (EG) followed by sonication for 30 min. Then, the reactant solution was put in a microwave reactor (XH-200A, Beijing XiangHu Science and Technology Development Co.) with the optimized heating program, 700 W (10 s) and 160 W (30 s) alternatively for 19 rounds. The product was filtrated to separate Pt-CQD/RGO solid. The Pt-CQD/RGO solid was washed with deionized (DI) water for several times before dried completely in vacuum oven. During experiments, we notice that the color of filtrate is light brown, which suggests part of CQD maybe dissolve in the filtrate. The dissolved CQD in a certain volume of the filtrate was separated by vacuum drying and weighed. Thus, the CQD remaining in the Pt-CQD/RGO product is calculated as 35 mg based on the total value in the filtrate and the mass of CQD added in reaction.

For comparison, GO or CQD and H2PtCl6 EG solution were subjected to the similar fabricating process with Pt-CQD/RGO to prepare Pt-RGO or Pt-CQD correspondingly. However, for CQD and H2PtCl6 solution, the system after microwave radiation is dark brown solution (Figure S1a). Very less solid can be collected on the filter papers and the filtrated solution is still dark brown, as shown by the optical image of the filter paper and the filtrated solution in Figure S1b and S1c correspondingly. Therefore, the possibility of investigating the structure and properties of Pt-CQD is excluded.
The Pt content in our catalysts was measured with direct Ultraviolet-Visible spectroscopy (UV-2550, Shimadzu) according to the process in the literature [30].

The structure was analyzed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, working at 10 kV), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100 (UHR); FEI, Tecnai G2 F20. Both operate at 200 kV), Raman microscope (Renishaw inVia, exciting wavelength: 514 nm) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Purkinje General MSAL XD-2 apparatus with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) scanning from 10° to 90°. The average size of Pt particles inferred from XRD pattern is based on the Scherrer equation and the Pt (220) peak. The fluorescence spectroscopy was acquired on a fluorometer from Horiba (FluoroMax-4).

The electrochemical tests of the as-prepared catalysts were conducted on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation. Pt wire and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) are the counter and reference electrode correspondingly.

2 mg of each catalyst and 20 μl of 5wt% Nafion 117 solution (Sigma Aldrich Co.) were dispersed in 0.4 ml DI water and sonicated for 30 min. Then 6 μl of the ink was carefully dropped onto the well-polished glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) and dried under an infrared lamp. The solution of all electrochemical experiments was purged with pure N2 for 15 min before starting test and N2 was bubbled into the solution during the whole process of tests. All potentials mentioned below are referenced to SCE. The detailed parameters of the electrochemical tests are given in the corresponding results part.

The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) is calculated as: ECSA = QH/(0.21 × mPt). QH (mC) is the charge calculated from the H desorption peak after corrected by the double layer area. 0.21 (mC cm−2) is the averaged value of the charge for oxidizing one monolayer of H atom on Pt surface. mPt is the Pt mass on GCE.

As the raw material of preparing catalysts, the as-synthesized CQD was first characterized by TEM and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Fig. 1 a is a typical TEM image of the CQD we prepared with the average size of single CQD determined to be 3.2 ± 0.6 nm. The CQD solution is observed as light yellow under the daylight lamp (Figure S2a) while it demonstrates intense green luminescence if irradiated by the UV light of 365 nm (Figure S2b). Fig. 1b provides the PL spectra of the CQD solution recording the fluorescence emission at 424 nm when excited at 362 nm. Based on the previous reported relationship between the PL emission and the size of CQD [31,32], the diameter of our CQD is estimated in the range of 3–4 nm which is coincided with the value subtracted from the TEM images.

The SEM micrograph of Pt-CQD/RGO in Fig. 2 a displays that the micrometer-size RGO sheets arrange into porous structure through edge folding, disordered stacking, random corrugation and ripples. In high resolution image of Fig. 2b, the uniform distributed layer of Pt nanoparticles can be recognized although the single Pt nanoparticle is not resolved due to its small size. More importantly, no big size Pt particles or apparent aggregation of initial particles are observed in the SEM experiments. No structural units with the reliable characteristics of CQD can be identified although great efforts were devoted in the repeated SEM measurements. CQD and RGO have similar chemical components, which leads to the similar contrast in SEM imaging. Therefore, the small size CQD may be totally drowned in the extreme strong signal of RGO frame. In contrast to the porous structure of CQD/RGO hybrids, the typical SEM image of Pt-RGO (Fig. 2c) manifests that the RGO supports are rather dense and compact because of the serious restacking of RGO sheets. Even worse, Fig. 2d discloses that the Pt nanoparticles in Pt-RGO are bigger and not dispersed on RGO surface homogenously (some large Pt particles are marked out with black circles in Fig. 2d).

The TEM results of Pt-CQD/RGO confirm the small size and uniform dispersion of Pt particles further. Fig. 3 a reveals that the distribution of Pt particles size is rather narrow with the average value of 2.7 ± 0.5 nm. Furthermore, most of the Pt particles adsorb on the supporting materials separately and no serious aggregation can be found in the Pt-CQD/RGO samples. In the center of some Pt particles, the hexagonal atomic lattice is resolved in high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3b). From Fig. 3b, it can be seen also that some of Pt particles deviate from the ideal round shape by being elongated more or less in certain direction. To help us identify the CQD component in Pt-CQD/RGO, the CQD/RGO material was prepared by heating the EG solution of CQD/GO mixture with the same parameters to that of Pt-CQD/RGO and investigated with TEM (Figure S3). The light dark contrast dots around 3 nm in Figure S3 can be quite safely attributed to CQD, which proves that CQD remain in our final Pt-CQD/RGO products indeed. Although CQD in the TEM images of Pt-CQD/RGO (Fig. 3b) is not as obviously as that in CQD/RGO (Figure S3) because of the heavy screening effect of the large contrast of Pt particles, it still can be discerned based on the hints revealed in Figure S3 if inspecting Fig. 3b carefully. Some CQD are circled out for clarity. Most of CQD attach to Pt particles, but not all. While for Pt-RGO, the Pt particle size is bigger (average size 4.5 ± 0.6 nm) and conglomeration is quite obviously, as shown by Fig. 3c and d.

XRD experiments were carried out to analysis the structure of Pt-CQD/RGO and Pt-RGO in another way. Fig. 4 a combines the XRD patterns of the two catalysts together. The peak at∼ 25° which is related to the (002) plane of graphite is quite broad and dispersive for Pt-CQD/RGO while it is much sharp and narrow for Pt-RGO, which implies the stacking structure of RGO sheets in Pt-RGO is more crystallized and more like graphitic structure. The peaks at ∼ 39.6°, 49.2°, 67.5°, 81.4° are attributed to the (111), (200), (220), (311) crystal planes of Pt in order. From Fig. 4a, we can also find that the diffraction peaks of Pt in Pt-CQD/RGO are wider than that of Pt-RGO, indicating the size of Pt in Pt-CQD/RGO is smaller. By the Scherrer equation and the data of Pt (220) peak, the size of Pt is calculated as 2.8 nm (Pt-CQD/RGO) and 3.8 nm (Pt-RGO). The structure features of Pt-CQD/RGO and Pt-RGO inferred from the XRD data are consistent with the topographic traits presented by the corresponding SEM and TEM images.

Raman spectroscopy was applied to investigate the properties of carbon supports in Pt-CQD/RGO and Pt-RGO. The spectra of GO and the two catalysts (Fig. 4b) are featured with the typical D and G peak. The intensity ratio of D/G peak (ID/IG) in Pt-CQD/RGO and Pt-RGO is about 1.3 and 1.0 correspondingly. Both of them is higher than that of GO (0.9) implying the successful reduction of GO to RGO [33]. Here, we need to point out that the larger ID/IG of Pt-CQD/RGO does not mean the RGO sheets in Pt-CQD/RGO are reduced better than that in Pt-RGO because we can not exclude the possibility that the defective surface structures of CQD may contribute to the signal of D peak also in Pt-CQD/RGO.

The Pt content in Pt-CQD/RGO and Pt-RGO is measured as 15% and 16% correspondingly by the method given in the experiments. Therefore the performance will be compared with the commercial 20% Pt/C (Alfa Aesar Co.) because of the same level of Pt content. Based on the information in the experimental section, the Pt mass loading on each cm2 of the work electrode is calculated as 0.064 mg cm−2, 0.068 mg cm−2 and 0.085 mg cm−2 for Pt-CQD/RGO, Pt-RGO and Pt/C respectively.

In the first step, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves for estimating electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) were scanned between −0.25 V and 1 V (vs SCE) in nitrogen-bubbled 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with the rate of 50 mV s−1. The well-defined peaks of H ad/desorption are observed in all the CV curves of Pt-CQD/RGO, Pt-RGO and Pt/C, as shown in Fig. 5 a. It can be seen apparently that Pt-CQD/RGO and Pt/C demonstrate much higher and broader H ad/desorption peaks than Pt-RGO, which indicates more active sites in Pt-CQD/RGO and Pt/C. Using the equation given in the experimental section, the ECSA value of Pt-CQD/RGO is determined as 58 m2 g−1, a little lower than that of Pt/C (86 m2 g−1). While for Pt-RGO, the average ECSA (20 m2 g−1) is much smaller. The poor ECSA of Pt-RGO is not a surprise because both the XRD and electron microscopy characterizations unveil the heavy restacking of RGO sheets and the apparent aggregation of the bigger-size Pt initial particles clearly.

The activities of three catalysts towards MOR were examined in nitrogen-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 +1 M CH3OH solution with a scanning speed of 50 mV s−1 at the range of −0.20 V and 1 V (vs SCE). Fig. 5b presents the typical CV curves normalized by the loading mass of Pt on the electrode. From the CV curves, it can be known that the onset potential of Pt-CQD/RGO is lower than that of Pt/C or Pt-RGO, revealing the higher activity of Pt-CQD/RGO. The oxidation peak in forward scan is considered as one of major parameters characterizing MOR activity of Pt catalysts. Very obviously disclosed in Fig. 5b, the mass activity (MA) of Pt-CQD/RGO at forward peak position (fp) is much better than that of Pt/C or Pt-RGO. The statistical value of MA(fp) for Pt-CQD/RGO is 529 mA mg−1 Pt, more than two times of the commercial Pt/C (254 mA mg−1 Pt) and three folds of Pt-RGO (165 mA mg−1 Pt). The ECSA of Pt-CQD/RGO is somewhat smaller than that of Pt/C, which rule out the possibility that the high MA of Pt-CQD/RGO originates from more reaction sites. The specific activity (SA) of Pt-CQD/RGO at forward peak position computed by the ECSA data and MA(fp) is 0.91 mA cm−2, roughly three times that of Pt/C (0.30 mA cm−2). (Because of the extreme small ECSA of Pt-RGO, it is not rational to compare the SA of Pt-RGO with that of Pt-CQD/RGO or Pt/C. Therefore, the following part will not consider the SA of Pt-RGO). Besides the activity at the forward peak position, the catalytic properties close to the fuel cell operating conditions are also great importance from the consideration of technological interest. Therefore, we compare the MA and SA at 0.3 V (vs SCE) as well. At this position, the MA of Pt-CQD/RGO is 86 mA mg−1 Pt, much higher than 54 mA mg−1 Pt of Pt/C and 26 mA mg−1 Pt of Pt-RGO. And the SA of Pt-CQD/RGO at this position, 0.15 mA cm−2, is more than two times bigger than that of Pt/C (0.06 mA cm−2). Therefore, it can be seen that the SA of Pt-CQD/RGO at both the forward peak position and 0.3 V (vs SCE) is greatly enhanced. A very recent reference [34] investigated the CO stripping on Pt/the controlled-reducing RGO sheets by electrochemical methods and density functional computation. Their researches clearly demonstrate that the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of supporting materials can downshift the onset potential of CO stripping and weaken the binding energy of CO on Pt surface. It is a widely accepted fact that the CO produced by the incomplete oxidation of methanol is the main substance of deteriorating the performance of Pt catalysts. The CQD synthesized with VC carries abundant varieties and quantities of oxygen groups on their surface (see the detailed discussions on Figure S5 and Table S2 in the last part of this section also.) and the TEM data demonstrate most of CQD are connected to Pt particles. Therefore, it can be inferred reasonably that CQD component is the main origin of the distinguished MA and SA of Pt-CQD/RGO.

Currently, the experimental parameter that is used to indicate the antipoisonous ability of Pt catalysts is controversial. Some research use the intensity ratio of forward/backward oxidation peak (If/Ib) [35–37], higher ratio means better antipoisonous ability. In our experiments, the statistical value of If/Ib for Pt-CQD/RGO is 1.6, much larger than that of Pt/C (0.9) and Pt-RGO (1.3). However, some researches demonstrate the backward peak is also the oxidation of methanol and propose that the antipoisonous ability should be judged by the steady-state current in chronoamperometric (I–t) curves [38]. For this reason, the I–t curves of Pt-CQD/RGO were acquired in 0.5 M H2SO4 +1 M CH3OH by fixing the potential at 0.50 V (vs SCE) for a duration of 3600 s and collated with that of Pt/C and Pt-RGO as well. Fig. 5c demonstrates that the initial current of Pt-CQD/RGO is much higher than that of the other two catalysts, which is consist with the vigorous activity shown in the CV curves of MOR in Fig. 5b. After the decaying period of I-t curves, the quasi-stable state is achieved for all the three samples. The inserted image in Fig. 5c exhibits that Pt-CQD/RGO yields bigger quasi-stable current than the other two catalysts. The MA of Pt-CQD/RGO at 3600 s is 4.7 mA mg−1 Pt, better than that of Pt/C (3.2 mA mg−1 Pt) and Pt-RGO (1.0 mA mg−1 Pt). And the SA of Pt-CQD/RGO at 3600 s is 0.0085 mA cm−2, more than two times of Pt/C (0.0037 mA cm−2). Thus all the parameters, If/Ib, the MA and SA at 3600 s of I-t curves, clearly show that the antipoisonous ability of Pt-CQD/RGO is greatly improved. Since the oxygen groups on the surface of supporting materials can reduce the adsorbing strength of CO on Pt surface [34], the superior antipoisonous feature of Pt-CQD/RGO is a natural result. Therefore, all the electrochemical testing results come to one point that the performance of Pt-CQD/RGO toward MOR is much more preferable to that of Pt/C and Pt-RGO because of the CQD component in the supporting materials.

Since CQD is the key factor to boost the fulfillments of Pt-CQD/RGO, we further investigated the effect of CQD content on the properties of the catalysts. 5 mg and 30 mg CQD were used to prepare Pt-CQD/RGO-5 and Pt-CQD/RGO-30 correspondingly while keeping the microwave heating parameters and the amount of GO and Pt precursor as same to that of Pt-CQD/RGO. In contrast to the light brown filtrate of Pt-CQD/RGO, The filtrate liquid of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 is transparent and that of Pt-CQD/RGO-30 is almost transparent, therefore we think that all of the added CQD remain in the final products.

The structure of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 and Pt-CQD/RGO-30 was characterized by the similar methods to that of Pt-CQD/RGO. The SEM image of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 (Fig. 6 a) reveals the irregular folding and stacking of RGO sheets. In contrast to the solid and dense structure of Pt-RGO (Fig. 2c and d), Pt-CQD/RGO-5 are loose and less compacted. However, TEM experiments (Fig. 6b) unveil the severe aggregation of Pt component in Pt-CQD/RGO-5. The statistical size of Pt in Pt-CQD/RGO-5 is measured as 4.1 ± 0.6 nm on the basis of the TEM images resolved single Pt particle, a little bit smaller than the Pt size in Pt-RGO (4.5 ± 0.6 nm). For Pt-CQD/RGO-30, the SEM (Fig. 6c) and TEM (Fig. 6d) measurements indicate that the features of the supporting substrate and the Pt particles are similar to that of Pt-CQD/RGO (Figs. 2a, b, 3a and b). The size of Pt particles measured from the TEM images of Pt-CQD/RGO-30 is 2.9 ± 0.5 nm, almost equivalent to that of Pt-CQD/RGO (2.7 ± 0.5 nm) considering the experimental errors. For comparing the Pt particle size of all catalysts (except Pt/C), the value of Pt particle size inferred from the TEM and XRD results (part of results are given below) is summarized in Table S1.

The XRD tests on Pt-CQD/RGO-5 and Pt-CQD/RGO-30 are provided in Figure S4a showing the dispersive graphite peak and the standard diffracting patterns of Pt particles. Pt-CQD/RGO-5 produces sharper and narrower Pt patterns than Pt-CQD/RGO-30 or Pt-CQD/RGO implying the bigger Pt particle size in this sample. The Pt particle size in Pt-CQD/RGO-5 is estimated as 3.6 nm, close to the value of 3.8 nm in Pt-RGO and the value of 2.7 nm in Pt-CQD/RGO-30 is almost equivalent to that of Pt-CQD/RGO (2.8 nm, see in Table S1). The Raman spectra of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 and Pt-CQD/RGO-30 in Figure S4b demonstrate the typical D and G peaks also with the ratio of ID/IG determined as 1.1 for both samples. The increasing ID/IG confirms the successful reduction of RGO also (the above discussion of Fig. 4b shows that ID/IG of GO is 0.9).

Fig. 7 provides the electrochemical results of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 (19% of Pt, the Pt loading on the work electrode is 0.081 mg cm−2) and Pt-CQD/RGO-30 (18% of Pt, the Pt loading on the work electrode is 0.076 mg cm−2) together with that of Pt-CQD/RGO to compare the achievements of the three catalysts conveniently. Pt-CQD/RGO-5 and Pt-CQD/RGO-30 show the typical H ad/desorption peaks in Fig. 7a and well-defined oxidation signals of methanol in Fig. 7b. To easily learn the catalytic data of all catalysts tested in this report and overview the changing tendency, the calculated value of ECSA, MA and SA at forward peak position, 0.3 V (vs SCE) and 3600 s of I-t curves (Fig. 7c) and If/Ib are listed in Table 1 (Because of the extreme small ECSA of Pt-CQD/RGO-5, the following part will not consider the SA of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 either).

Pt-CQD/RGO-5 displays similar level of activities, either for the MA at forward peak or 0.3 V (vs SCE), to that of Pt-RGO because the enhancing effect of small amount of CQD is quite limited. For Pt-CQD/RGO-30, the MA at forward peak and 0.3 V (vs SCE) is 413 mA mg−1 Pt and 73 mA mg−1 Pt, both of them are noticeably improved as referring to 179 mA mg−1 Pt (forward peak) and 37 mA mg−1 Pt (0.3 V vs SCE) of Pt-CQD/RGO-5. The ECSA of Pt-CQD/RGO-30 is also much larger than that of Pt-CQD/RGO-5. The quasi-stable current in the I-t curves of Fig. 7c drops in the sequence of Pt-CQD/RGO, Pt-CQD/RGO-30 to Pt-CQD/RGO-5 (see the data of 3600 s in Table 1), implying the descendant antipoisonous ability from Pt-CQD/RGO, Pt-CQD/RGO-30 to Pt-CQD/RGO-5. Analyzing the parameters of Pt-CQD/RGO-30 or Pt-CQD/RGO with that of Pt-CQD/RGO-5 or Pt-RGO, it can be recognized evidently that the optimization of the catalytic properties is rooted in the existence of CQD in the final product.

As above mentioned, the oxygen groups on the surface of supporting materials play an important role in the catalysis process of MOR. Therefore, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization on GO, Pt-RGO, Pt-CQD/RGO-5, Pt-CQD/RGO-30 and Pt-CQD/RGO is performed with the spectra given in Figure S5. The inferred data from the XPS analysis are listed in Table S2, including the content of total oxygen, sp2/sp3 graphitic carbon (CC/C–C), hydroxyl/phenolic group (C–OH), epoxide group (C–O–C), carbonyl group (CO) and carboxyl group (COOH) in the respective tested sample. The total oxygen content in the reduced samples drops down reasonably in comparing with GO. Among the four reduced samples, Pt-CQD/RGO-5 shows a much lower data of oxygen content in the repeating XPS measurements. This result is somehow unexpected but understandable because many previous researches have proved the active reduction ability of varied CQD in sorts of reactions although the studied CQD could be fabricated by different raw materials and methods [39–41]. Therefore, the RGO sheets in Pt-CQD/RGO-5 are possibly reduced more completely by the added small amount of CQD than the one in Pt-RGO, which results the lower level of oxygen content in Pt-CQD/RGO-5. For Pt-CQD/RGO-30 and Pt-CQD/RGO, the oxygen groups from the excess amount of CQD raise the total oxygen content again. The activities of Pt-RGO and Pt-CQD/RGO-5 are deteriorated largely by the big size and severe aggregation of Pt particles and the heavy restacking of RGO sheets, as can be seen from the Figs. 2c and d, 3c and d, 6b and Table S1. While for Pt-CQD/RGO-30 and Pt-CQD/RGO, CQD benefits the activities of Pt in several sides: small size CQD and the single bond oxygen groups on the CQD surface provide a large amount of nucleating and anchoring points [34], which facilitates the formation of small uniform Pt nanoparticles and prevent the aggregation of Pt particles; CQD may help RGO sheets arranging into porous structure by acting as spacers, and the double-bonded oxygen groups on the CQD surface will promote the antipoisonous abilities of Pt components [34].

In this report, a series of high efficiency Pt catalysts were prepared by one pot reducing GO, Pt salts and a designed amount of CQD with ethylene glycol and their performance towards MOR were tested by electrochemical methods. The results reveal that CQD can enhance the fulfillments of the Pt catalysts greatly indeed. Among all, the CQD-saturated catalyst (Pt-CQD/RGO) exhibits best mass and specific activities both at forward peak position and the position close to fuel cell operation, best antipoisonous ability and long term stability. Through analyzing the structure and electrochemical data of the catalysts with different CQD content, we suggest that CQD provide multiple benefits for the activity booming: help to form small size Pt particles, prevent the aggregation of Pt particles and RGO sheets, and promote the antipoisonous ability through the synergistic effect between the plentiful oxygen groups on its surface and Pt nanoparticles. The research is of significant importance to understand the relationship between the supporting materials and the performance of Pt-related catalysts, improve the mass and specific activities of Pt catalysts by tuning the surface properties of supporting substrates, develop high performance fuel cell catalysts and reduce the cost of the corresponding fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2015.10.092.
